text: Microbes and Health (M&H) - Title Recent advances reveal a lot of host–microbe interactions for the intestinal microbiota that is involved in gut–brain signaling. Thus, the present study aims at exploring the effects of intestinal microbiota on cerebral metabolism using a model comprising germ-free (GF) mice and their ex-GF mice derivatives which were subsequently introduced with the fecal suspension derived from specific pathogen-free (SPF) mice. Using capillary electrophoresis with time-of-flight mass spectrometry (CE-TOFMS) on mouse brains, the authors identified a set of 196 metabolites from the cerebral metabolome in GF and ex-GF mice. Further comparisons indicated that the levels of 38 metabolites in mouse brain differed significantly between the two groups (p < 0.05). Among them, about 10 have been demonstrated to be involved in the process of brain function. The roles of other metabolites to which their related physiological functions remain to be validated still remain unknown. Most importantly, this study unexpectedly explored a newly observed link between cerebral glycolytic metabolism and intestinal microbiota. The authors suggested for the first time that host cerebral metabolites would be modified by the presence of normal intestinal microbiota in terms of the newly described microbiota–gut–brain axis, and that the microbiota related with the host brain function and pathology at various levels, such as development, attenuation, learning and memory as well as behavior.
explanation: Plausible scientific style with domain terms; some odd phrasing suggests AI generation, but it’s also compatible with human-written abstracts.
